coredata.dc:title: Green microwave-prepared N and S Co-doped carbon dots as a new fluorescent nano-probe for tilmicosin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Elshenawy, Eman A.
El-Malla, Samah F.
Hammad, Sherin F.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: A straightforward, rapid, and selective fluorescent probe for determination of tilmicosin has been developed based on novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (NS-CD). The NS-CDs were synthesized, for the first time, through green, simple one step microwave pyrolysis in only 90 s using glucose as carbon source and l-cysteine as nitrogen and sulfur source. This proposed synthesis method was energy-efficient and resulted in NS-CDs with high production yield (54.27 wt%) and narrow particle size distribution. Greenness of NS-CDs synthesis method was assessed using EcoScale and was proven to be excellent green synthesis. The produced NS-CDs were applied as a nano-probe for determination of tilmicosin in its marketed formulation and milk based on dynamic quenching mechanism. The developed probe showed a good performance for tilmicosin detection in marketed oral solution and pasteurized milk and linearity range of 9–180 μM and 9–120 μM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tilmicosin
Microwave-assisted synthesis
N,S co-doped carbon dots
Green preparation
Dynamic quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pharmaceutical formulation and milk sample analysis
Assessment of the greenness of the synthesis method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the NS-CDs, (B) HRTEM of the NS-CDs; inset: lattice spacing of 0.29 nm and (C) SAED pattern of the NS-CDs.
UV, excitation, and emission spectra of the prepared NS-CDs.
Stern–Volmer plots for the quenching of NS-CDs fluorescence by TLM at three different temperatures.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of NS-CDs, TLM, a mixture containing NS-CDs and TLM (with DW as a blank) and a mixture containing NS-CDs and TLM (with NS-CDs as a blank).
Fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TLM (0, 9, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 240, 270 μM).

Response of NS-CDs towards TLM and possible interferants (lactose, ascorbic acid, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, PO4 3−, and propylene glycol); [Co2+] = 50 μM and concentration of other interferants = 100 μM.
Greenness assessment of proposed synthesis method by EcoScale tool.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: > 75, excellent; >50, acceptable; and <50, inadequate environmental impact.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Validation parameters of the proposed probe for TLM detection.
Application data of the proposed probe for assay of TLM in pharmaceutical formulation.
Application of the proposed probe for TLM assay in milk.

head.ce:title: Green microwave-prepared N and S Co-doped carbon dots as a new fluorescent nano-probe for tilmicosin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta, 31111, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward, rapid, and selective fluorescent probe for determination of tilmicosin has been developed based on novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (NS-CD). The NS-CDs were synthesized, for the first time, through green, simple one step microwave pyrolysis in only 90 s using glucose as carbon source and l-cysteine as nitrogen and sulfur source. This proposed synthesis method was energy-efficient and resulted in NS-CDs with high production yield (54.27 wt%) and narrow particle size distribution. Greenness of NS-CDs synthesis method was assessed using EcoScale and was proven to be excellent green synthesis. The produced NS-CDs were applied as a nano-probe for determination of tilmicosin in its marketed formulation and milk based on dynamic quenching mechanism. The developed probe showed a good performance for tilmicosin detection in marketed oral solution and pasteurized milk and linearity range of 9–180 μM and 9–120 μM, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tilmicosin (TLM) is a bacteriostatic macrolide antibiotic with a broad spectrum activity that is only meant for veterinary use [1]. It works by attaching to the 50 S ribosomal subunit and inhibiting protein synthesis [2]. It has been widely used to treat a variety of infections brought on by bacteria and mycoplasma in livestock and it is the drug of choice for respiratory infections in broiler chickens [3]. TLM is also used as a feed additive to lower the incidence of disease and increase feed utilization rate, hence promoting growth [4]. On the other hand, TLM residues in food, that result from the improper and excessive use of veterinary medicine, can endanger human health by triggering hypersensitivity, toxicity, allergenicity, dysbacteriosis, and bacterial resistance [5–7]. As a result, the European Medicines Agency and Codex Alimentarius Commission have set TLM's maximum residue limits for milk and broiler chicken's muscles and liver at 50 μg/L, 150 μg/kg and 2400 μg/kg, respectively [8,9]. At present, chromatographic methods [10–21] and immunoassays [9,22,23] are the primary applied methods for the determination of TLM and no spectrofluorimetric methods were reported for its determination. Therefore, constructing a simple, rapid, affordable, and eco-friendly spectrofluorimetric technique for TLM determination is crucial.

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as new class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials of particle sizes in the range of 2–10 nm [24]. Owing to their remarkable properties, such as being easily synthesized, abundant, inexpensive, and non-toxic, CDs have recently gained a lot of interest [25]. CDs can be easily altered to adjust their optical, electronic, and surface properties for selected purposes [26]. Doping with heteroatoms, like boron, nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, and silicon, is considered as the most promising modification method. Due to the different electronegativity of heteroatoms from carbon and hydrogen and possible lone pairs of electrons, heteroatom doping alters the electronic structure of the nanoparticle and consequently the band gaps between energy levels [27]. Additional functionality is imparted to the doped CDs by heteroatom doping as well.

There are a number of suggested methods to synthesize CDs, but the microwave-assisted technique stands out for a number of reasons, including its simplicity, environmental friendliness, speed, affordability, better product yields (PY), and enhanced particle size uniformity [28–31].

In this study, a green, simple one step microwave preparation of a novel sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs (NS-CDs) utilizing glucose as a carbon source and l-cysteine as nitrogen and sulfur dopant was described for the first time. The implied preparation method was fast, requiring only 90s, energy-saving, and produced a high PY. An important feature of the proposed synthesis method is being an excellent green synthesis as evaluated by the EcoScale. The produced NS-CDs were applied to create straightforward, quick, and selective spectrofluorimetric method for determination of TLM for the first time. The developed probe was applied for TLM detection in its marketed formulation and milk based on dynamic quenching mechanism.

l-cysteine hydrochloride anhydrous was purchased from Acros organics (USA). Glucose, calcium chloride, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate, ferrous chloride tetrahydrate, sodium chloride, magnesium sulfate, cobalt acetate, and propylene glycol were obtained from ISO-Chem (France)., Ascorbic acid, sodium acetate, sodium hydroxide, boric acid, lactose monohydrate, and ammonium acetate were purchased from ADWIC company (Egypt). Quinine Sulfate was purchased from Alpha Chemika (India). Hexane, chloroform, glacial acetic acid, and conc HCl (reagent grade) were purchased from AdventChembio (India). Methanol was purchased from Fisher Scientific (USA). TLM (assay, 98.15%) was gifted by ATCO Pharma for Pharmaceutical Industries (Egypt). Pulmox® 25% oral solution was purchased from a commercial veterinary retailer. Pasteurized milk was purchased from the local market.

Fluorescence measurements were done using JASCO FP‐6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). Hettich benchtop centrifuge (Germany), Branson 3510 ultrasonic cleaner (USA) and domestic microwave oven (1000 W) were utilized during NS-CDs synthesis and sample preparation. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were performed utilizing JEOL JEM-2100 HRTEM worked at 200 kV accelerating voltage (Japan). UV absorption spectra were acquired on Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Japan). FTIR plots were acquired on Jasco FT/IR-4100 spectrophotometer (USA). Calibrated HANNA pH211 pH‐meter (USA) was employed for pH measurement.

Microwave-assisted preparation was employed as follows: glucose (0.174 g, 0.966 mmol) and l-cysteine (0.025 g, 0.159 mmol) were heated in domestic microwave (90 s) until obtaining dark orange residue. Once the product is cooled to room temperature, 10 mL distilled water were added to dissolve the residue and were ultrasonicated for 20 min before being filtered using 0.22 μm syringe filter.

The prepared NS-CDs’ PY was estimated as described by Zheng et al. [32]. The solution of NS-CDs was lyophilized to attain NS-CDs powder, and PY value was determined applying formula below using the solid's weight.
P Y =
(
m
C D s
/
m s
)
× 100
where
m
C D s
is NS-CDs solid's weight, and
m s
is the starting materials' weight.

The QY was computed using the equation below:
Q Y
x
=
Q Y
s t
(
I x
/
I
s t
)
(
A
s t
/
A x
)
(
η x 2
/
η
s t
2
)

The symbols “st” and “x” indicates quinine sulfate (reference fluorophore; prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4) and NS-CDs, respectively.
η
is refractive index of solvent (1.33 for both), A is value of absorbance at 350 nm, and I denotes integrated fluorescence following excitation at 350 nm [33,34]. Absorbance was maintained not more than 0.1 to decrease the absorption effect and QYst was 0.54.

To prepare TLM stock standard solution (0.9 mM), 0.078 g was dissolved in 10 mL methanol, and then diluting 1 mL of this solution to 10 mL with distilled water. Various volumes of TLM stock solution were added to 50 μL of 10 mg/mL NS-CDs, diluted to 3 mL with distilled water, mixed to obtain solution covering the working concentration range (9–180 μM), and fluorescence was determined at 416 nm (λex = 350 nm). The same process was used to assess the proposed method's selectivity for TLM over potential interference-causing elements. 5 nm bandwidth was maintained for both emission and excitation monochromators, and a 1000 nm/min scanning speed was used for all fluorescence recordings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pharmaceutical formulation and milk sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: For oral solution analysis, 3 mL of Pulmox® solution were diluted with distilled water to 100 mL, then 1 mL of this solution was diluted to 10 mL with the same solvent. A volume of 300 μL of resulting solution was measured following procedure of TLM assay.

For milk assay, milk samples were spiked with TLM and mixed well to obtain sample solutions with concentration range of 9–120 μM and the sample preparation was performed according to Parker and Patel [35]. Typically, 10 mL of spiked milk was extracted with 30 mL methanol using vortex for 10 min, then sonication for 5 min, centrifugation (10 min, 6000 rpm) and supernatant was gathered. The residue was resuspended in 30 mL methanol, the extraction was repeated, and the supernatants were combined. A volume of 50 mL sodium chloride (10 g%) was combined with the supernatant and 1 M HCl solution was used to adjust the pH to 2.5 and the lipophilic impurities were removed by extraction with 50 mL hexane. TLM was recovered from methanol by adjusting the pH to 9.0 with 1 M sodium hydroxide solution and then extracting with two portions of 30 mL chloroform. The organic layer was evaporated till dryness and the residue was dissolved in 2 mL methanol, sonicated for 1 min, and completed to 10 mL with distilled water. A volume of 3 mL of this solution was added to 50 μL NS-CDs in the cuvette and measured following the procedure for TLM assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the greenness of the synthesis method
ce:section.ce:para: Elimination or reduction of hazardous substances are the core of green chemistry when designing chemical products and processes [44–47]. Based on the twelve principles of green chemistry, many analytical tools were proposed for evaluating the logical outcomes of these principles in different applications [48–52]. One of the analytical tools that is used for evaluation of the synthesis process is the EcoScale [53] which assesses yield, cost, safety, environmental factors, and simplicity of workup/purification to determine the quality of organic preparation. The suggested method relies on allocating various penalty points to these factors and subtracting them from the ideal reaction EcoScale value of 100 [53,54]. Greenness of the proposed CDs’ synthesis was estimated using EcoScale and demonstrated EcoScale value of 75.14 indicating an excellent environmental impact (Table 1 ).

In the pH range of 3.0–11.0, the impact of pH on the fluorescence quenching of NS-CDs at a concentration of 0.166 mg/mL by TLM (150 μM) was investigated. There is no substantial variation in fluorescence intensity ratio of NS-CDs, so no buffer was employed in subsequent experiments (Fig. S6). Following the addition of TLM (150 μM) to the NS-CDs (0.166 mg/mL), the impact of incubation period on fluorescence quenching was investigated (Fig. S7). For 20 min, the fluorescence intensity was monitored every minute and it was discovered that the interaction between TLM and NS-CDs is quick and completes within a minute. For 30 min, the probe's signal remained consistent.

TLM significantly decreased the fluorescence of the NS-CDs. The potential quenching mechanism was explained using Stern-Volmer formula:
F 0
/ F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]

considering F and F
0 as the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CDs in presence and absence of TLM, respectively, [Q] is TLM concentration while K
SV stands for Stern-Volmer quenching constant [55]. At three distinct temperatures, fluorescence quenching efficiencies (F
0
/F) were investigated (Fig. 3 ). The K
SV increased with increasing temperature showing a dynamic quenching mechanism which involve collision between TLM and the excited state of CDs causing its return to the ground state (K
SV equals 4.70 × 103, 5.75 × 103 and 8.26 × 103 L mol−1 at 303, 313 and 323 K, respectively). In addition, no changes appeared in the absorption spectra of NS-CDs because dynamic quenching affects only the excited state of CDs. As seen in Fig. 4 , no new substance was created, and UV spectrum of the mixture of NS-CDs and TLM is the sum of both. This was demonstrated by the fact that the spectrum of the mixture matched that of TLM alone when the mixture's NS-CD concentration was used as a blank.

The NS-CDs were employed to detect TLM at a concentration of 0.166 mg/mL, and quantitatively, their fluorescence decreased with increasing TLM concentration (Fig. 5 ). With r2 value of 0.997, the plot of TLM concentration against fluorescence quenching (F
0
–F) demonstrated linearity in the 9–180 μM range (Fig. S8). The QL and DL were estimated using the equations
10
σ
/ S
and
3.3
σ
/ S
, where S is the slope of linear calibration curve while
σ
is SD of the intercept of a regression line around QL and DL, respectively. The intercept, slope, and SD were evaluated from regression analysis (Table 2 ).

The devised probe was utilized to analyze TLM in Pulmox® oral solution (25 g TLM/100 mL). Following the steps described under “Pharmaceutical formulation and milk sample analysis”, sample solutions were prepared and evaluated. The results were shown in Table 3 and statistical comparisons were made with the results of the comparison method [11]. The statistical analysis demonstrated how consistently the probe determined TLM.

The detection of TLM in milk was carried out to further evaluate the applicability and reliability of the proposed NS-CDs probe. Milk samples were processed with a 92% percentage extraction using the procedures described under “Pharmaceutical formulation and milk sample analysis”. Following that, samples were examined using calibration curve created under the same treatment circumstances by adding small aliquot of prepared milk samples into the proposed probe and measuring fluorescence intensity. With the regression equation y = 1.126x + 2.703 (r2 = 0.995), the calibration curve of TLM in milk was linear over the range (9–120 μM). The results in Table 4 show that the probe applied to measure TLM in milk has good accuracy and precision.

To serve as a new probe for TLM sensing, a novel fluorescent nano-probe was successfully created for the first time using a green, straightforward, fast, and affordable microwave synthesis of NS-CDs, using glucose and l-cysteine. In-depth investigation of the structural and optical properties of NS-CDs was conducted using a variety of techniques. The greenness of the proposed synthesis method for NS-CDs preparation was evaluated using EcoScale tool and was demonstrated to have an excellent environmental impact. TLM quantitatively quenched the fluorescence of the prepared probe through dynamic quenching leading to quick response and stable fluorescence signals. Thus, this probe was successfully applied as the first fluorescence-based TLM detection in pharmaceutical formulation and milk samples.

Eman A. Elshenawy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing - Original Draft; Samah F. El-Malla: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - Review and Editing; Sherin F. Hammad: Resources, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - Review and Editing; Fotouh R. Mansour: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - Review and Editing.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
